facts.text: College basketball in the United States is played primarily under the NCAA and is one of the country's most passionate amateur sports scenes.
March Madness is the nickname for the NCAA men's and women's basketball tournaments each spring.
The men's NCAA tournament field expanded over time into the modern 68-team format.
The women's NCAA tournament has grown rapidly in visibility, attendance, and TV ratings in the 2020s.
The Final Four refers to the last four teams remaining in the NCAA tournament.
A 'Cinderella' team is a lower-seeded school that makes an unexpected tournament run.
UCLA's dynasty under coach John Wooden won 10 national championships in 12 years, including seven in a row.
Kentucky, North Carolina, Kansas, Duke, and UConn are among the most tradition-rich programs in college basketball.
The three-point line was introduced to men's NCAA basketball in 1986, permanently changing offensive strategy.
The shot clock was adopted in college basketball to prevent teams from stalling and to increase pace.
One Shining Moment plays at the end of the men's NCAA tournament broadcast and has become an annual tradition.
Bracket pools are a huge part of March Madness culture, even for people who do not watch the sport all season.
Historic upset stories like Villanova over Georgetown in 1985 helped build the tournament's legend.
College basketball arenas are known for intense student sections, rivalries, and loud home-court atmospheres.
Conference tournaments can give underdog teams an automatic NCAA bid even if their regular season was uneven.
The sport has become a major pipeline to the NBA and WNBA, but many legendary college stars are remembered just as much for their NCAA careers.
March Madness became the nickname for the NCAA basketball tournaments and their annual drama.
The phrase now defines one of the most famous stretches in American sports.
